1: 'Ask rain from the LORD in the season of the spring rain, from the LORD who makes the storm clouds, and he will give them showers of rain, to everyone the vegetation in the field.' The call to seek YHWH for rain continues the theme of seeking him for all provision (Zechariah 7-8). In the agricultural context of ancient Israel, rain — especially the latter rain (geshem malkosh, spring rain in March-April, crucial for ripening grain) — was the most immediate test of divine blessing and the most natural arena for prayer. YHWH is the cloud-maker (oseh chazizim), not the Baal religion's gods of storm (Ps 29: YHWH controls the storm).
2: 'For the household gods utter nonsense, and the diviners see lies; they tell false dreams and give empty consolation. Therefore the people wander like sheep; they are afflicted for lack of a shepherd.' The contrast with pagan divination: the terafim (household idols/gods, H8655) speak nonsense (dibru aven), the diviners see lies (chazah sheker), the dreamers speak vain comfort. Without true prophetic guidance, the people wander like sheep without a shepherd — a profound image of spiritual and social disorientation. The shepherd theme (central in Zechariah 10-11) is introduced: the people need a true shepherd because the false ones have failed.
3: 'My anger is hot against the shepherds, and I will punish the leaders; for the LORD of hosts cares for his flock, the house of Judah, and will make them like his majestic steed in battle.' YHWH's anger targets the shepherds (ro'im) — the political and religious leaders who have failed to shepherd the people. The punishment of the leaders (atudim, he-goats = strong leaders) who failed their flock is followed by YHWH's direct care for the flock. The transformation of the flock: from sheep wandering without a shepherd (v2) to 'his majestic steed in battle' (sus hoddo bemilchamah) — YHWH transforms the weak into the powerful.
4: 'From him shall come the cornerstone, from him the tent peg, from him the battle bow, from him every ruler — all of them together.' The 'him' is Judah (v3 ends with 'house of Judah'): from Judah shall come the four titles of leadership: (1) pinna (cornerstone) — the key architectural element (cf. Isaiah 28:16; Psalms 118:22; Matthew 21:42); (2) yated (tent peg) — the fixed point of stability (Isaiah 22:23); (3) qeshet milchamah (battle bow) — military strength; (4) yotze kol nogesh yachdav (every ruler together) — comprehensive leadership. The NT applies the cornerstone (pinna) to Christ (Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6).
5: 'Together they shall be like mighty men in battle, trampling the foe in the mud of the streets; they shall fight because the LORD is with them, and they shall put to shame the riders on horses.' The empowered Judah/Israel: fighting like mighty men because YHWH is with them. 'Trampling the foe in the mud of the streets' — the humiliation of once-proud enemies. 'Put to shame the riders on horses' — cavalry defeated by infantry, the natural order of military strength inverted by YHWH's presence. The war-horse is cut off (Zechariah 9:10) but the horsemen are defeated by YHWH's empowered people.
6: 'I will strengthen the house of Judah, and I will save the house of Joseph. I will bring them back because I have compassion on them, and they shall be as though I had not rejected them, for I am the LORD their God and I will answer them.' The reunification oracle: both Judah (southern kingdom) and Joseph/Ephraim (northern kingdom) will be restored. 'As though I had not rejected them' (ki hayiti lo zenavitim) — the profound statement of complete forgiveness: not just restoration but a return to the status ante of full covenant relationship, as if the rejection never happened. This is the OT anticipation of Paul's 'no condemnation' (Romans 8:1) — the slate wiped clean.
7: 'Then Ephraim shall become like a mighty warrior, and their hearts shall be glad as with wine. Their children shall see it and be glad; their hearts shall rejoice in the LORD.' The northern tribes (Ephraim = northern Israel) restored: fighting as a mighty warrior (gibbor Ephraim), hearts glad as with wine (yismach libbam kayayin), children seeing and rejoicing. The intergenerational joy — both the current generation and their children seeing YHWH's restoration — is the completeness of the restoration promise.
8: 'I will whistle for them and gather them in, for I have redeemed them, and they shall be as many as they were before.' YHWH whistling/signaling (ashreqah lahem, H8319 — to hiss/signal, the way a shepherd calls his flock) to gather the scattered. The redemption vocabulary: ki feditim (for I have redeemed them, from fadah = to ransom, H6299) — the Exodus-redemption language applied to the gathering of the exiles. 'As many as they were before' (verabu kaasher rabu) — the demographic restoration, population returning to the pre-exile levels.
9: 'Though I scattered them among the nations, yet in far countries they shall remember me, and with their children they shall live and return.' YHWH scattered Israel (azra'em bagoyim) — the exile was YHWH's act. But the scattering was not the last word: from the far countries (bammerchaqqim) they shall remember (yizkeruni) YHWH. Memory of YHWH in the diaspora is the first movement of return — the mind turning back before the body returns (cf. Luke 15:17: he came to himself, remembering the father's house). Children born in diaspora will live and return.
10: 'I will bring them home from the land of Egypt, and gather them from Assyria, and I will bring them to the land of Gilead and to Lebanon, till there is no room for them.' The ingathering from Egypt (south) and Assyria (north) — the two poles of ancient Near Eastern power that had dominated Israel. Gilead (Transjordan) and Lebanon (north) represent the full territorial expansion of the restored kingdom. 'Till there is no room for them' (velo yimmatze lahem) — the land will be too full, an overcrowding of blessing. The contrast with the current desolation (Zechariah 7:14: no one went to and fro).
11: 'He shall pass through the sea of troubles and strike down the waves of the sea, and all the depths of the Nile shall be dried up. The pride of Assyria shall be laid low, and the scepter of Egypt shall depart.' The new Exodus imagery: YHWH striking the sea (Exodus 14:21-22), drying up the Nile (Isaiah 11:15 — the tongue of the Egyptian sea), overthrowing the two great empires (Assyria and Egypt) that embody all world-power. The pattern of the Exodus (sea-crossing, Egyptian defeat) is the template for the eschatological ingathering.
12: 'I will make them strong in the LORD, and they shall walk in his name, declares the LORD.' The ultimate goal of the ingathering: not merely physical restoration but spiritual strengthening — vegibartem baADONAI (I will make them strong/mighty in the LORD). Walking in YHWH's name (yithaleku bishmo) is the NT's walking 'in Christ' (Colossians 2:6), walking 'in the Spirit' (Galatians 5:16). The restored people's identity is defined by YHWH's name, not their ethnicity or geography.
